coredata.dc:title: N-aminomorpholine-functionalized bromine-doped carbon dots for hypochlorous acid detection in foods and imaging in live cells
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Chengda
Yuan, Pengxiang
Wang, Dan
Li, Shiyao
Yao, Huan
Yang, Liu-Pan
Wang, Li-Li
Du, Fangfang
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorous acid (HClO) is used in food preservation. However, excessive HClO can deteriorate nutritional composition of food, compromise its quality, and potentially induce various diseases. Consequently, the development of multifunctional fluorescent probes for the sensitive and selective detection of HClO is highly anticipated for food safety. In this work, we designed a nanoprobe using N-aminomorpholine (AM)-functionalized bromine-doped carbon dots (Br-CDs-AM) for sensing HClO. This nanoprobe exhibits pH stability, strong resistance to photobleaching, superior long-term photostability (12 weeks), high sensitivity (19.3 nM), and an ultrarapid response (8 s) for detecting HClO residues in food matrices with percentage recovery (96.5 %–108 %) and RSDs less than 5.34 %. In addition, extremely low cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility enable the nanoprobe to be used primarily for lysosome tracking and rapidly visualizing HClO in live cells. Thus, this study provides a new pathway to design unconventional nanoprobes for food safety assessment and subcellular organelle-specific imaging HClO.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bromine-doped carbon dots
N-aminomorpholine-functionalized
Fluorescent probe
HClO sensing
Food safety
Lysosome-targetable imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of HClO in samples
Sensing performances of Br-CDs-AM to HClO
Biocompatibility and in vitro cell imaging of Br-CDs-AM

National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174059 ), the Scientific Research Project of Hunan Provincial Health Commission (Grant No. D202313027525 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province ( 2022JJ40363 ), and the Excellent Youth Funding of Hunan Provincial Education Department ( 22B0460 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Br-CDs (A) and Br-CDs-AM (C); size distribution of the as-synthesized Br-CDs (B) and Br-CDs-AM (D).
XPS survey scan of (A) Br-CDs and (D) Br-CDs-AM; the high-resolution C1s (B) and Br3d (C) of Br-CD; and the high-resolution C1s (E) and N1s (F) of Br-CDs-AM.
(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of Br-CDs, AM, and Br-CDs-AM. (B) FL spectra of Br-CDs-AM with different λex at 300–400 nm. (C) Optimal excitation and emission FL spectra.

(A) Fluorescence titration spectra of Br-CDs-AM in the presence of HClO (0–360 μM). Inset: The FL intensities of Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) as a function of HClO concentration. (B) Absorbance spectrum of Br-CDs-AM sample, HClO aqueous solution and Br-CDs-AM/HClO. (C) Fluorescence decay of Br-CDs-AM in the absence and presence of HClO. The λex/λem are set as 360 nm/464 nm. (D) Response of FL intensity of Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) after the addition of HClO (0.5 mM) or other different metal ions (0.5 mM) or anion ions (0.5 mM).

Confocal fluorescence images of A549 cells incubated with 2 mg mL−1 Br-CDs-AM and different concentrations of HClO (0, 50, 200 and 300 μM) in blue channels with the excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Emission was collected in the blue channel (410–480 nm). (I) Mean fluorescence intensities of live cells (E, F, G and H). Error bars represent mean deviation (±S.D.) n = 5, scale bar = 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Fluorescence microscopy images of RAW264.7 cells. The control groups (A and D) are incubated with Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) for 3 h. The cells in groups (B and E) are first incubated with LPS (1 μg mL−1) for 12 h, followed by coincubation with PMA (1 μg mL−1) and Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) for 3 h. The cells in groups (C and F) are first incubated with LPS (1 μg mL−1) for 12 h, then treated by NAC (500 nM) for 1 h, followed by coincubation with PMA (1 μg mL−1) and Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) for 3 h, λex = 405 nm, λem = 410–480 nm (blue channel), (G) Mean fluorescence intensities in live cells (D–F). Error bars represent mean deviation (±S.D.), n = 5, scale bar = 50 μm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Detection of HClO in food samples (n = 6).

head.ce:title: N-aminomorpholine-functionalized bromine-doped carbon dots for hypochlorous acid detection in foods and imaging in live cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Scheme 1 Illustration of the synthetic procedure of Br-CDs-AM and the application in sensing HClO in food matrices and lysosomal imaging.

Hypochlorous acid (HClO) is used in food preservation. However, excessive HClO can deteriorate nutritional composition of food, compromise its quality, and potentially induce various diseases. Consequently, the development of multifunctional fluorescent probes for the sensitive and selective detection of HClO is highly anticipated for food safety. In this work, we designed a nanoprobe using N-aminomorpholine (AM)-functionalized bromine-doped carbon dots (Br-CDs-AM) for sensing HClO. This nanoprobe exhibits pH stability, strong resistance to photobleaching, superior long-term photostability (12 weeks), high sensitivity (19.3 nM), and an ultrarapid response (8 s) for detecting HClO residues in food matrices with percentage recovery (96.5 %–108 %) and RSDs less than 5.34 %. In addition, extremely low cytotoxicity and outstanding biocompatibility enable the nanoprobe to be used primarily for lysosome tracking and rapidly visualizing HClO in live cells. Thus, this study provides a new pathway to design unconventional nanoprobes for food safety assessment and subcellular organelle-specific imaging HClO.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Hypochlorous acid (HClO) plays a vital role in immune defense against intracellular pathogen invasion (Feng et al., 2018), which is sometimes added as an antibacterial agent in raw milk, poultry products, and vegetables to prevent microbial growth. It has been reported that the common fraudulent behavior in milk involves adding HClO as a preservative, which prevents the emergence of odor, taste, and viscosity changes, and extends the product’s shelf life as a result of chemical reactions between HClO and milk components (Melo et al., 2014). The reactions neutralize the medium and eventually lower growth of microorganisms (Melo et al., 2014). However, excess HClO in raw milk can oxidize proteins and lipids, damaging certain nutrients and thus posing a threat to human health, such as causing strong irritation to the skin and mucous membranes, or inducing methemoglobinemia (Guerreiro, 2018). Therefore, the Chinese national food safety standard sets the maximum residue limit of HClO in food matrices at 0.97 μM, whereas the European Union prohibits its use in meat-related products (Fu et al., 2021). In this case, it is urgent to develop a sensitive, fast and economical analytical technique for determination of HClO residues in food matrices. In addition, HClO is an important reactive oxygen species (ROS) in living organisms. It can be endogenously generated by myeloperoxidase (MPO) from chloride and H2O2 within phagosomes and participates in many physiological and pathological processes. ROS play essential roles in cell signaling and homeostasis, such as in aging, pathogen response, and anti-inflammation regulation (Yuan et al., 2015). Phagosomal HClO as a highly potent oxidant, kills pathogens in the innate immune system, and nonphagosomal intracellular HClO (nphHClO) helps suppress inflammation and regulate cellular apoptosis (Yuan et al., 2015). Nevertheless, persuasive evidence has demonstrated that excessive production of endogenous HClO induces oxidative injuries of nucleic acids, proteins, and lipids, leading to diseases such as kidney disease, lung injury, and even cancer (Yin et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2018). Overproduced HClO can also induce lysosomal rupture and cell death (Reiners et al., 2011). Thus, maintaining an appropriate level of HClO within the range of 10−5–10−2 M is crucial for some cellular functions (Cheng et al., 2014). Therefore, it is highly imperative to monitor and control HClO levels in vivo.

Although traditional analytical methods such as electrochemical techniques, high-performance liquid chromatography and colorimetric methods have been used to detect HClO (Zhong et al., 2023), the fluorescence (FL) probes-detection technique is always preferred for the visualization of HClO due to its noninvasive and in situ real-time imaging capabilities, sensitivity, selectivity, ease of operation and cost-effectiveness (Hou et al., 2022). To date, many FL probes such as small organic molecules (Gu et al., 2019), fluorescent proteins (Zhi & Qian, 2021), and nanomaterials (Liu et al., 2023) have been established for sensing HClO in vitro, in vivo and in food samples. Nevertheless, some of these fluorescent probes suffer from low photobleaching resistance, relatively complicated synthesis processes, low sensitivity, biotoxicity, as well as poor organelle targeting ability (Chen et al., 2016; He et al., 2016). Therefore, it is desired to develop fluorescent probes with excellent optical performance and organelle targeting ability for the determination of HClO in complex biological and food samples.

Carbon dots (CDs) have been regarded as a carbon-based 0-dimensional luminescent nanomaterials with outstanding properties, including anti-photobleaching, biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, low toxicity, and easy to be functionalized (Wang & Lu, 2022; Wang et al., 2022). These characteristics render CDs attractive for versatile applications, including quantitative detection, bioimaging, nanomedicine, optoelectronic devices, etc (Doring et al., 2022; Jia et al., 2022; Shen et al., 2020; Xu et al., 2020). CDs can be designed from readily available precursors through straightforward and robust processes. This offers new synthetic opportunities via tailored CD synthesis or post-functionalization protocols, advancing applications such as bioimaging application and sensing for reporting the residual levels of various risk factors in food matrices (Inderbir et al., 2023). For instance, Wang et al. reported a CDs-based sensor was fabricated by covalently connecting the carboxyl group of naphthalimide derivative with the amino group on the surface of CDs for monitoring lysosome formaldehyde in HeLa cells (Chen et al., 2019). Wu’s group reported red-emitting CDs for the selective detection of brilliant blue FCF in food additives with an acceptable detection limit of 84 nM (Wu et al., 2023). These studies are instructive for the construction of CDs with terminal functional groups for specific applications. Although several CDs-based fluorescent probes have been designed for a ClO− response, they had low sensitivity (Yang et al., 2023) and slow response time (Wang et al., 2018), mainly focusing on cell or in vivo imaging (Cheng et al., 2023; Meng et al., 2022), with a very rare concern for their applications in food safety assessment. Therefore, we hypothesized that constructing highly sensitive and responsive functionalized CDs may achieve multifunctional applications for the detection of HClO in food matrices and intracellular HClO imaging.

Herein, Br-CDs were synthesized using a one-pot hydrothermal method to carbonize the only precursor of bromothymol blue. Subsequently, a novel lysosome targeting nanoprobe was developed through a nucleophilic substitution reaction between the amino group of N-aminomorpholine (AM), a morpholine derivative targeting lysosomes and the Br- group on bromine-functionalized carbon dots (Br-CDs) to sensitively detect HClO. The as-prepared Br-CDs-AM displayed a fluorescence turn-off response to HClO over other biologically active molecules based on a static quenching mechanism with a wide linear range (0.1–360 μΜ) and high sensitivity (limit of detection (LOD) = 19.3 nM), and rapid response (<10 s), making it a desirable platform for assessing residual HClO-triggered food safety risks. In contrast with iodometric titration method in accordance with the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 22990-2008), the Br-CDs-AM-based analysis method showcases the following innovative aspects: (1) Br-CDs-AM is capable of ultrarapidly responding to HClO. (2) Br-CDs-AM achieved quantification of HClO with high sensitivity and high selectivity. Moreover, Br-CDs-AM can localize to lysosomes and visualize fluctuations in both exogenous and endogenous HClO levels in A549 and RAW264.7 cells.

Bromothymol blue, N-aminomorpholine, and other reagents were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corporation (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of AR grade, which were not further processed, unless otherwise noted.

First, bromothymol blue (0.01 g) was dissolved in 15 mL of ethanol solution. After 10 min of ultrasonic treatment, the solution was heated at 200 °C for 12 h in 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave, yielding a faint yellow solution with blue fluorescence. Once naturally cooled to room temperature, the supernatant was decanted from the autoclave, and subjected to rotary evaporation. The resulting sticky residue was dispersed in deionized (DI) water and centrifuged at a relative centrifugal force of 7424 × g for 15 min. Finally, the supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis bag (MWCO: 1000) to remove excess molecules, followed by lyophilization to obtain a pale brown powder.

At first, 0.005 g of Br-CDs solid, 40 μL of N-aminomorpholine, and 160 μL of triethylamine were combined in a 50 mL round-bottomed flask and dissolved in 16 mL of acetonitrile. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 6 h and then evaporated to remove the acetonitrile. The residue was dialyzed against ultrapure water using a dialysis bag (MWCO, 1000 Da) to remove unreacted N-aminomorpholine, followed by freeze-drying to yield Br-CDs-AM solid.

A stock solution of Br-CDs-AM was diluted in PBS buffer (pH 7.4) to a final concentration of 2.0 mg/mL. Then, 50 μL of different concentrations of HClO solution was added to 1.95 mL of the diluted solution and systematically mixed. The selectivity experiment was conducted by measuring the FL intensities of Br-CDs-AM in the presence of HClO, various biologically active species such as ROS (H2O2, O2 •− and •OH), RNS (ONOO−, NO2 −, and NO3 −), reactive sulfur species (RSS) (glutathione (GSH), l-cysteine (l-Cys) and SO3 2−), other anions (Cl−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2− and CO3 2−), and cations (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+). The FL spectra were recorded under an excitation of 360 nm at a scan rate of 1200 nm min−1. The slit widths for excitation and emission were set at 10 nm.

The LOD and limit of quantification (LOQ) were estimated using the 3 s and 10 s criteria, respectively: 3 and 10 times the standard deviation of the blank, divided by the sensitivity (slope of the calibration curve). The ratio (F0 − F)/F0 (where F0 and F are the FL intensities of Br-CDs-AM in the absence and presence of HClO, respectively) was plotted against the corresponding concentration to obtain a linear equation within the range of 0.1–360 μM. Method recovery experiments were conducted to establish overall accuracy (i.e., “trueness”) and precision.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment and analysis of HClO in samples
ce:section.ce:para: The milk, yogurt, and pork samples were purchased from a local supermarket with two replicates for each food type, and then treated based on our previous reports (Jia et al., 2022). 50.0 μL of trichloroacetic acid solution was added to 5.0 mL of milk, yogurt, or an aqueous solution containing 0.03 g of minced pork, followed by sonication for 20.0 min. The mixture was then centrifuged at 8734 × g for 15 min. The pH of the supernatant was adjusted to 7, and subsequently, the supernatant was diluted to 100 mL. Br-CDs-AM solution was added to these diluted samples as control group, followed by spiking with standard HClO solution (10, 30 or 50 μM). Six replicates were prepared for each spiked concentration (each matrix had three replicates, and each replicate had two spiked technical replicates). Emission intensities at 464 nm were recorded for all samples.

For colocalization imaging, A549 cells were first stained in DMEM medium with Br-CDs-AM at a concentration of 2 mg mL−1 for 3 h. The cells were washed twice with 10 mM PBS solution at pH 7.4, then treated with Lyso-Tracker Red DND-99 (LTR, 50 nM). After 15 min, the cells were rinsed using PBS and imaged on LSM880 CLMS. Excitation was carried out using lasers at λex = 405 or 568 nm, and with emissions recorded in the 420–480 or 610–712 nm wavelength ranges, respectively.

For FL imaging of exogenous HClO, A549 cells were treated with 2 mg mL−1 Br-CDs-AM for 3 h, and rinsed with PBS (pH 7.4) to remove excess Br-CDs-AM. The cells were then incubated with 1 mL of DMEM containing different concentrations of HClO (0, 50, 200 and 300 µM) for another 30 min before imaging with an LSM880 CLMS (λex = 405 nm, λem = 410–480 nm).

For endogenous HClO imaging, RAW 264.7 cells were seeded in 1 mL of DMEM containing 10 % FBS and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. Cells were incubated with Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) for 3 h as a control group. For the experimental group, cells were stimulated by lipopolysaccharide (LPS) (2 μg/mL) for 12 h, followed by co-incubation with 1 μg mL−1 of phorbol myristate acetate (PMA) and 2 mg mL−1 of Br-CDs-AM for 3 h. Cells were washed with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to remove uninternalized drugs prior to imaging. Another group was first cultured with LPS (1 μg mL−1) for 12 h, and treated with N-acetylcysteine (NAC) (500 nM) for 1 h, further incubated in medium containing PMA (1 μg mL−1) and Br-CDs-AM (2 mg mL−1) for 3 h, and finally imaged on an LSM880 CLMS.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images revealed that the as-prepared Br-CDs and Br-CDs-AM were spherical-shaped, and well-dispersed (Fig. 1 A, C). Br-CDs-AM with an average particle size of 4.07 nm ± 0.4 was slightly larger than Br-CDs with a particle size of 3.64 ± 0.50 nm, indicating that Br-CDs were successfully cross-linked with AM (Fig. 1B, D).

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to characterize the surface functional group composition of Br-CDs and Br-CDs-AM. The full-range XPS curve of Br-CDs corroborated that there were four elements, including Br (76. 8 eV), S (168.0 eV), C (298.9 eV), and O (545.8 eV) (Fig. 2 A). The C 1s spectral peaks can be deconvoluted into two peaks for CC/CC (284.8 eV) and CO (286.6 eV) (Fig. 2B). The Br 3d spectrum can be fitted into two peaks with binding energies of 70.4 eV and 71.3 eV (Fig. 2C), which corresponds to the characteristics of Br 3d 3/2 and Br 3d 5/2, suggesting the presence of Br in Br-CDs samples (Wu et al., 2019). After Br-CDs reacted with AM, the nitrogen element appeared in the XPS survey spectra of Br-CDs-AM (Fig. 2D). The high-resolution spectrum of N1s exhibits two main peaks at 399.9 and 401.0 eV, which was attributed to CN and morpholine N (Fig. 2E) (Huang et al., 2019). Furthermore, a new peak appeared at 285.1 eV in C1s spectrum of Br-CDs-AM, which was assigned to CN, proving that AM was introduced into Br-CDs by substitution reaction (Fig. 2F) (Wu et al., 2017).

Similar observations were made from FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. S1). For Br-CDs, the absorption band located at 3446 cm−1 was ascribed to the stretching vibration of OH. The peaks of free AM have been assigned to the bending vibration of the primary amine at 1612 cm−1, CN stretching vibration at 1464 cm−1, and COC stretching vibration at 1113 cm−1. After the functionalization of Br-CDs with AM, a new peak emerged at 3198 cm−1 in Br-CDs-AM, and the band at 1612 cm−1 vanished (Nie & Fan, 2021), suggesting that AM reacted with Br-CDs via the nucleophilic substitution reaction (Zhang et al., 2020). In addition, the functional groups of CN and COC on AM were also observed on the surface of Br-CDs-AM. The appearance of characteristic absorption peaks at 1663 cm−1 can be attributed to the CC stretching vibration of Br-CDs, further confirming the successful linkage between AM and Br-CDs. The 1H NMR spectrum of AM displayed two distinct groups of peaks at 3.74 and 2.76, respectively (Fig. S2). After functionalization with AM, Br-CDs-AM exhibited two prominent new groups of peaks similar to those of AM, indicating the successful connection of Br-CDs to AM.

As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the UV–vis spectra of Br-CDs-AM show two obvious peaks at 248 nm and 273 nm, which were attributed to blue shifts of the π–π* transition of Br-CDs at 260 nm and the absorption peak of AM at 288 nm, respectively. The fluorescence spectra of Br-CDs-AM show excitation-dependent features, with optimal emission at 464 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 3B, C). The fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of Br-CDs and Br-CDs-AM were calculated to be 10.7 % and 9.7 %, respectively (Fig. S3).

The effect of xenon lamp radiation, ionic strength, and pH on the FL of Br-CDs-AM was evaluated in detail. The fluorescence intensities of Br-CDs-AM remained almost invariable under varying radiation time (from 0 to 72 min) and KCl concentration (from 0 to 2.5 M), verifying that Br-CDs-AM has excellent photobleaching resistance and salt tolerance (Fig. S4A and Fig. S4B). Br-CDs-AM exhibited a relatively stable FL intensity in the pH range of 2–12, suggesting its suitability as a superior optical imaging agent in the acidic environment of lysosomes (Fig. S4C). Moreover, the long-term fluorescence stability of Br-CDs-AM was investigated. The FL intensity of Br-CDs-AM remained at approximately 88 % of the initial FL after 12 weeks of storage at 4 °C (Fig. S4D), indicating excellent long-term photostability in this system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performances of Br-CDs-AM to HClO
ce:section.ce:para: The nanoprobe with a fast time response is conducive to mapping short-lived HClO in live cells. The time-dependent FL changes of Br-CDs-AM toward HClO were recorded. As shown in Fig. S5A, the FL intensity of Br-CDs-AM at 460 nm was rapidly quenched upon the addition of HClO (approximately 8 s), and remained nearly unchanged after 40 s. This result suggests that Br-CDs-AM is exceptionally rapid nanoprobe for monitoring HClO fluctuation. In addition, the influence of pH on the fluorescent response of Br-CDs-AM to HClO was evaluated. As shown in Fig. S5B, the fluorescence intensity of the Br-CDs-AM remained stable at pH 4.0–6.0, and decreased slightly as pH increased from 7.0 to 12.0. Upon adding HClO to the PBS solution at pH 2.0–12.0, a notable fluorescence quenching of Br-CDs-AM was observed across the pH ranges, demonstrating that the assay is suitable for most intracellular pH environments.

The sensitivity of Br-CDs-AM as a nanoprobe was assessed by introducing various concentrations of HClO into the aqueous solution of Br-CDs-AM. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the FL of Br-CDs-AM at 464 nm progressively decreased with increasing HClO concentrations (0–360 μM). The inset exhibits a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency (F0 − F)/F0 and the concentrations of HClO from 0.1 to 360 μM. The linear equation was [(F0 − F)/F0 = 0.04601 + 0.00206C], with a R2 of 0.999. The LOD was calculated to be 19.3 nM using the formula LOD = 3σ/slope, which is lower than that of previously reported CDs-based nanoprobes (Liu et al., 2020; Simoes et al., 2020). The limit of quantification (LOQ) was determined to be 67.79 nM, according to the formula LOQ = 10σ/slope, demonstrating adequate trueness and precision (Table S1). Compared to other CDs-based nanosensors for HClO detection, this nanoprobe exhibits a faster response time, a wider linear range, and higher sensitivity (Table S2).

The quenching mechanism of Br-CDs-AM toward HClO was explored through UV–vis spectroscopy and FL lifetime measurements. HClO exhibited a characteristic peak at 292 nm (Fig. 4B). After the addition of HClO, the absorption bands of Br-CDs-AM at 248 and 274 nm decreased, accompanied by the emergence of a new peak at 380 nm (Fig. 4B). This phenomenon demonstrates that HClO can form non-covalent compounds with Br-CDs-AM. Furthermore, FL lifetime measurement was performed to confirm the FL quenching mechanism. The average lifetime values of Br-CDs-AM were 5.623 ns (χ2 = 1.102) and 5.606 (χ2 = 1.173) before and after the introduction of HClO (Table S3). The negligible change in the FL lifetime of Br-CDs-AM in the presence of HClO (Fig. 4C), provides further evidence for static quenching owing to the complex formation between Br-CDs-AM and HClO.

Taking into account the complexity of the biological environments, the FL responses of Br-CDs-AM toward potential interfering substances such as ROS (H2O2, O2 •−, and •OH), RNS (ONOO−, NO2 −, and NO3 −), RSS (GSH, l-Cys, and SO3 2−), other anions (Cl−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, and CO3 2−), cations (Na+, K+, Cu2+, Zn2+, and Mg2+) and HClO at a concentration of 0.5 mM were investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4D, HClO induced a significant FL quenching of Br-CDs-AM, while other potential interferences had a negligible effect on the FL intensity of Br-CDs-AM. Moreover, the interference study of Br-CDs-AM for HClO detection in the presence of the aforementioned interfering substances revealed that the nanoprobe Br-CDs-AM can still respond to HClO quickly and accurately (Fig. S6). These results demonstrate that as-prepared Br-CDs-AM has excellent selectivity and outstanding anti-interference properties toward HClO.

The repeatability and reproducibility conditions were determined in spiked samples. Milk, yogurt and pork samples were spiked at concentrations of 10, 30, and 50 μM, and analyzed six times (Table S4). Trueness and precision under repeatability conditions were calculated as average recoveries of 95.74 %–99.41 % and relative standard deviations (RSD) of 2.59 %-5.13 %. The trueness and precision under reproducibility conditions were acquired six different days within a month period, the average recoveries were 95.74 %–99.41 % and the RSD of six repeatability trueness values were 3.43–6.32 %. This degree of precision is well within acceptable ranges for quantitative analyses.

The feasibility of the Br-CDs-AM nanoprobe for sensing HClO in samples (milk, yogurt and pork) was confirmed via the standard addition method. As indicated in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 96.5 % to 108.0 % with RSDs less than 5.34 % in all trials. This confirms the practical utility of Br-CDs-AM nanoprobe for analyzing HClO in food matrices. The performance of our developed nanoprobes for the determination of HClO in actual samples was compared with several reported methods (Table S5). The LOD achieved by this method is superior to electrochemical and surface-enhanced Raman scattering methods. Although the LODs of the colorimetric method are of the same order of magnitude as this method, the Br–CDs–AM nanoprobe is sensitive enough for the detection of HClO in milk, yogurt, and pork with a rapid response.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biocompatibility and in vitro cell imaging of Br-CDs-AM
ce:section.ce:para: Before cell imaging experiments were performed, the cytotoxicity was evaluated using the MTT assay in RAW264.7 cells. Cell viability exceeded 94 % at high concentrations (600 μM) of Br-CDs-AM similar to Br-CDs (Fig. S7), demonstrating low toxicity and suitability of Br–CDs–AM as a nanoprobe for imaging in living cells. To confirm the subcellular localization of Br-CDs-AM in live cells, colocalization experiments were conducted using the commercial lysosome tracker, Lyso-Tracker Red. As displayed in Fig. S8, the fluorescence of Br-CDs-AM evidently overlapped with Lysol-Tracker red with a colocalization coefficient reaching up to 0.87, suggesting that Br-CDs-AM targets lysosomes effectively due to the morpholine group.

Encouraged by the in vitro behavior of Br-CDs-AM, the efficacy of Br-CDs-AM for visualizing HClO in live cells was further studied. As illustrated in Fig. S9, cells stained with Br-CDs-AM initially displayed prominent blue fluorescence. However, the introduction of HClO into cells pretreated with Br-CDs-AM, rapidly weakened blue fluorescence, stabilizing after approximately 9 min. This suggests that HClO can quickly enter cells and interact with Br-CDs-AM. Subsequently, Br-CDs-AM was employed to image exogenous HClO in A549 cells. As can be seen from Fig. 5 , A549 cells exhibited intense blue FL after treatment with Br-CDs-AM. When cells were preincubated with increasing doses (0, 50, 200 and 300 μM) of HClO, the fluorescence markedly attenuated, providing experimental evidence that Br-CDs-AM could effectively map exogenous HClO in A549 cells. Finally, the application of Br-CDs-AM in monitoring intracellular endogenous HClO was investigated. In Fig. 6 , the observed decrease in the fluorescence signal in RAW264.7 cells was primarily owing to the interaction between Br-CDs-AM and endogenous HClO generated by PMA and LPS stimulation. This hypothesis, was tested by treating LPS/PMA-stimulated RAW264.7 cells with NAC to reduce intracellular HClO levels (Zhan et al., 2017). Compared to cells stimulated with LPS/PMA alone, the fluorescence of the cells was recovered to some extent after the addition of LPS/PMA/NAC, indicating the capability of CDs-AM visually track endogenous HClO levels in RAW264.7 cells.

In summary, a novel nanoprobe (Br-CDs-AM) was synthesized via a substitution reaction between amino groups on N-aminomorpholine and Br groups on the surface of Br-CDs for quantitative analysis of HClO. This sensing platform offers high photostability, lysosome-targeting capability, and a reliable approach for ultrarapid and precise quantification. These characteristics endow them with a good prospect of applications. It can serve as a highly selective and fast-responding fluorescent nanosensor for the detection of HClO in food samples, and can specifically locate to lysosomes for imaging of HClO in living cells. This study demonstrates that Br–CDs–AM is a promising tool for food safety monitoring and for studying HClO-associated physiological processes.

Chengda Zhao: Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Pengxiang Yuan: Formal analysis, Methodology, Visualization. Dan Wang: Methodology. Shiyao Li: Methodology. Huan Yao: Software. Liu-Pan Yang: Conceptualization. Li-Li Wang: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Fangfang Du: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22174059), the Scientific Research Project of Hunan Provincial Health Commission (Grant No. D202313027525), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ40363), and the Excellent Youth Funding of Hunan Provincial Education Department (22B0460).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.138284.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
Description of characterization, MTT assay of Br-CDs and Br-CDs-AM, FL quantum yield calculations; FTIR and1H NMR spectra of AM, Br-CDs and Br-CDs-AM; photostability, time-dependent fluorescence response, effect of different pH on HClO sensing, comparison of some reported fluorescence CDs and other methods for the detection of HClO, lifetime data, trueness/precision data, interference test data, FL spectra of CNDs in different pH, and MTT assay data, confocal microscopy fluorescence colocalization images, dynamic LSCM images.
